passages.explanation: Paul commends Phoebe, a deacon of the church in Cenchreae, and asks them to receive her in the Lord in a way worthy of his people and to give her any help she may need. She has been the benefactor of many people, including Paul himself. Phoebe is likely the one carrying this letter to Rome. Paul calls her a 'deacon' (the same Greek word used for male deacons) and a 'benefactor' or 'patron' — a term implying significant social standing and financial support. The most important letter in Christian theology was hand-delivered by a woman.

Paul greets a long list of people in Rome — remarkable for someone who's never visited. He names Priscilla and Aquila, his co-workers in Christ Jesus who risked their lives for him. He greets the church that meets in their house. He greets Epenetus, the first convert in the province of Asia. He greets Mary, who worked very hard for them. He greets Andronicus and Junia, his fellow Jews who've been in prison with him — they are outstanding among the apostles and were in Christ before him. He greets Ampliatus, Urbanus, Stachys, Apelles, Aristobulus' household, Herodion, those in the household of Narcissus who are in the Lord, Tryphena and Tryphosa, Persis, Rufus and his mother, Asyncritus, Phlegon, Hermes, Patrobas, Hermas, Philologus, Julia, Nereus and his sister, Olympas, and all the Lord's people who are with them. He tells them to greet one another with a holy kiss. All the churches of Christ send greetings. The list is a window into the early church: men and women, Jews and Gentiles, slaves and free, named as co-workers, fellow prisoners, and hard workers. About a third of the names are women. Junia is called 'outstanding among the apostles' — a woman recognised as an apostle. The diversity of names (Greek, Latin, Jewish) reflects Rome's cosmopolitan church.

Paul urges them to watch out for those who cause divisions and put obstacles in their way that are contrary to the teaching they've learned. Keep away from them. Such people are not serving our Lord Christ but their own appetites. By smooth talk and flattery they deceive the minds of naive people. Everyone has heard about the Romans' obedience, so Paul rejoices because of them. He wants them to be wise about what is good and innocent about what is evil. The God of peace will soon crush Satan under their feet. The grace of our Lord Jesus be with you. After twenty-six names and warm greetings, Paul inserts a sharp warning about divisive false teachers. The contrast between genuine co-workers (the long greeting list) and self-serving deceivers is pointed. The promise that God will crush Satan under 'your feet' echoes Genesis 3:15 — the Roman believers participate in the victory.

Timothy, Paul's co-worker, sends greetings, as do Lucius, Jason, and Sosipater, Paul's fellow Jews. Tertius, who wrote down this letter, sends his greetings in the Lord. Gaius, whose hospitality Paul and the whole church enjoy, sends greetings. Erastus, the city's director of public works, and Quartus send greetings. Paul closes with a doxology: now to him who is able to establish you in accordance with my gospel, the message I proclaim about Jesus Christ, in keeping with the revelation of the mystery hidden for long ages past but now revealed and made known through the prophetic writings by the command of the eternal God, so that all the Gentiles might come to the obedience that comes from faith — to the only wise God be glory forever through Jesus Christ! Amen. Tertius gets his own greeting — the scribe briefly steps out from behind the pen. The closing doxology circles back to the letter's opening themes: the gospel promised in Scripture, the mystery now revealed, the Gentiles called to faith. The letter that began with 'the obedience that comes from faith' ends with the same phrase. Everything between those bookends — sin, justification, sanctification, Israel, ethics, unity — serves that single purpose.
